name: auth-method
title: Choose the authentication approach
description: Decide how users authenticate: password, OAuth providers, magic links, passkeys, SSO. Drives compliance posture, UX, and which integrations are needed.

starter.issue: Auth has a long tail: password reset, MFA, session management, account recovery. Choose a path early so account state and access control follow consistently.
assumptions: User type is understood (consumer, enterprise, internal).
Compliance requirements (SOC2, HIPAA, GDPR) are known.
constraints: Enterprise customers may require SAML SSO.

positions.title: Hosted auth (Clerk / Auth0 / WorkOS)
positions.description: Outsource user mgmt to a SaaS. Drop-in UI and APIs.
pros: SSO and MFA in the box

positions.title: Self-hosted auth library (NextAuth / Lucia / better-auth)
positions.description: Run auth yourself with a battle-tested library.
pros: No external dependency in critical path

positions.title: Passwordless only (magic links + passkeys)
positions.description: Email magic links and/or WebAuthn passkeys, no passwords stored.
cons: Less familiar UX for some users

positions.title: OAuth providers only (Google/GitHub/etc.)
positions.description: Federate to social/identity providers — no in-house auth.

implications: Determines session model (JWT vs DB-backed sessions).
Determines mailer dependency if magic links are used.
Drives compliance work: cookie banners, privacy policy, data-export tooling.
Account-deletion flow needs to be specified.

notes_for_agent: Ask: consumer or B2B? Will enterprise customers demand SSO/SAML in 6 months? Is the team comfortable owning auth surface? For B2B SaaS aiming at enterprise, hosted auth (Clerk/WorkOS) is usually right. For tiny side projects, OAuth-only is usually fine. If passkeys are on the table, validate device support assumptions.
